The `%skip` magic has been added, removed, added again, and it might be time to remove it again because of its inherit problem of removing output of marked cells. After discussing with the Jupyter developers (https://github.com/jupyter/help/issues/175), it appears that some upstream change (e.g. a `locked` tag) would be a much better way to prevent one or entire notebook from changed by cell execution.

issue_title: Rerun of completed tasks with missing output
issue_body:
A seeming simple scenario: Run a script with task, task completed. Remove output files, re-run, and sos will find that task already completed. In this case,

1. `sos run -s default` will still check signature and reaches the `task` statement because of signature mismatch.
2. underlying `sos execute task -s default` will refuse to run a completed task.
3. `sos run -s force` will rerun the step and task, but innocent steps/tasks will also be rerun.

This is a new problem with the new task format because previously sos has a `signature-mismatch` status for tasks. That is to say, when we check if a task is completed, it will check if the signature matches, so `sos execute task -s default` will execute if the signature mismatch.

Now, before we restore this `signature-mismatch` status, we need to think what this status means.

This status essentially binds the `task` file with its outputs, so a task would be considered failed whenever the input or output files are moved or removed. Considering you have a large number of jobs on the cluster, it is almost inevitable that input output files will be moved around after jobs are completed. 

There are also technical difficulties in getting this status. Because input and output files are stored outside of task files. We will have to read a task file, get its signature, and check input output files, potentially even calculate md5 of all these files. Considering doing this for ten thousand jobs that you have done in the past month each time you check the status of jobs? We can of course cache the status but we still need to check a lot of files to at least see if their timestamp has been change between each `sos status` check.

Now related to #1015 if we are re-executing a step with lots of jobs, jobs with now removed output files will be rerun, which is not acceptable in many cases.

So we are somehow stuck here with #1015...

issue_title: ZR300 crashes at startup when plugged in before booting computer
issue_body:
| Required Info  |   |
|---|---|
| Camera Model | ZR300 | 
| Camera Firmware Version | 2.0.71.28  | 
| Operating System & Version |  Ubuntu 16.04.2 |
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)  |  4.4.0-77-generic  |
| Build System |  Makefile | 

In case the sensor is plugged into the computer before the computer boots up, the sensor crashes with the following error when launched:

```
[ INFO] [1494502600.596998307]: Initializing nodelet with 8 worker threads.
[ERROR] [1494502601.036582722]: /camera/driver - Error calling rs_get_device_info ( device:0xadce80, info:ADAPTER_BOARD_FIRMWARE_VERSION ): 
selected camera info is not supported for this camera! 

[FATAL] [1494502601.178728447]: Failed to load nodelet '/camera/driver` of type `realsense_camera/ZR300Nodelet` to manager `nodelet_manager'
[camera/nodelet_manager-2] process has died.
```

Note that it does not happen after a restart but only after a complete shutdown.
Any ideas on how to fix this?

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
| Required Info                  |                                                   |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                        | Any |
| Firmware Version                    | Any |
| Operating System & Version   | Ubuntu 14.04 | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    | 4.4.0 | 
| Platform                                   | Pentium |
| SDK Version                            | 2.14.1 |
| Language                                | C++ |

### Issue Description
As mentioned in previous issues (#1428 #1443, #1464, #1484), there have been issues executing AVX instructions on CPUs that don't support AVX (causing SIGILL, "Illegal instruction (core dumped)"). Using SDK version 2.14.1, the issue is two-fold:

1. If I compile with `-mavx`, my application crashes at global initialization. I see that there is auto-selection code to bypass the AVX implementation if not supported on a CPU, but in debugging, I saw crashes in two different locations (one in librealsense and one in another library). Generally speaking, adding `-mavx` gives the compiler free-reign to optimize using AVX wherever it wishes, so I don't think this should always be enabled for non-ARM builds.

2. If I remove `-mavx`, the build fails due to SSSE3 guards (which my processor supports) where AVX guards should be used instead. I have a patch for this, which is ultimately the solution to my issue. I'll send a pull request with this patch shortly.

We've received numerous requests for S3 event support, but we never had a tracking issue for it.  As we start to flesh out sources for lambda events, I wanted to make sure we have a consistent approach.

I propose adding an `on_s3_event()` decorator, and that all future event sources use the `on_<service>_<something else>` naming convention.  The input parameter is a small abstraction over the event payload to support the most common use case: downloading the object from S3.

Because we don't yet support managed resources (https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/516), I initially wanted this first version to support pre-existing S3 buckets.  That is, you tell us what existing bucket you want us to configure, and we'll configure the appropriate s3 bucket notifications for you (along with the needed policy updates).  Here's a complete example of the famous s3 image resizer:

```python
from chalice import Chalice

import boto3

app = Chalice(app_name='s3resize')
s3 = boto3.client('s3')


@app.on_s3_event(bucket='my-preexisting-bucket')
def resize_image(event):
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile('w') as f:
        s3_client.download_file(event.bucket, event.key, f.name)
        resized_file = f.name + '.thumbnail'
        with open(Image.open(f.name)) as image:
            image.thumbnail(tuple(x / 2 for x in image.size))
            image.save(resized_file)
        s3_client.upload_file(filename=resized_file,
                              bucket='my-preexisting-dest-bucket',
                              key='%s-thumbnail' % event.key)
```

That's the entire thing.  You run `chalice deploy`, and assuming the source and dest bucket exists, everything just works.

A few things to note in this example:

* There's an optional `filters` and `events` kwarg in the decorator.  The default `events` is `s3:ObjectCreated:*` so it's omitted from the example.
* The `event` param has a `.bucket` and `.key` attribute to make it easy to download objects from s3.  Note this is assuming a single record is sent in the event payload.

## Concerns

The biggest concern is that due to us not managing the S3 bucket for you, you have to use a pre-existing bucket, which means that the **SAM template generation won't work**.  The notification configuration is a property of an `AWS::S3::Bucket` which means you *have* to create the S3 bucket as part of your CFN template.  I see a few possible options here:

1. Just don't support `on_s3_event` in SAM templates, generate a helpful error message.  This feature would only be available via `chalice deploy`.
2. "Make it work" by using custom CFN resources.  I haven't done a whole lot of research on this, other than to confirm that this is a viable approach.  I'd need to think this through more.  It also feels a bit odd leaving a lambda function/custom resource lying around.  This has a lot of risk and uncertainty, so it would probably delay this feature being released.
3. Add preliminary support for resources (#516).  We'd start with an S3 bucket, and we'll need to do another pass through the original proposal.  You could then say something like:


```python

@app.resource('s3.Bucket')
class MyBucket:
    name = 'some-bucket-name'

@app.on_s3_event(bucket=MyBucket)
def resize_image(event): pass
```

In this example, we'd also create the S3 bucket for you.  Even if we did option (3), we'd still need to decide whether or not to support `chalice deploy` working with pre-existing S3 buckets.  The only benefit of (3) is that it gives people a path forward that rely on the `chalice package` command, you just have to be ok with us managing the S3 bucket for you.

FWIW I have most of this implemented.  We just need to decide on which of the above options we want to go with for `chalice package`.  Let me know what you all think.

cc @stealthycoin @kyleknap

Something to consider:

> HDF5 has been evolving for many years now. By default, the library will write objects in the most compatible fashion possible, so that older versions will still be able to read files generated by modern programs. **However, there can be performance advantages if you are willing to forgo a certain level of backwards compatibility.** By using the “libver” option to File, you can specify the minimum and maximum sophistication of these structures:
> 
> ```python
> >>> f = h5py.File('name.hdf5', libver='earliest') # most compatible
> >>> f = h5py.File('name.hdf5', libver='latest')   # most modern
> ```
> 
> Here “latest” means that HDF5 will always use the newest version of these structures without particular concern for backwards compatibility. The “earliest” option means that HDF5 will make a best effort to be backwards compatible.
>
> The default is “earliest”.

http://docs.h5py.org/en/latest/high/file.html#version-bounding

I'm more than slightly annoyed by the vagueness of "a certain level of  backwards compatibility", but ok. Anyway, I'm bringing this up because according to [these slides about h5py usage by the NERSC](https://www.nersc.gov/assets/Uploads/H5py-2017-Feb23.pdf), using `libver='latest'` results in 2.25x better performance for them. Could end up quite significant if the loom files increase in size in the future.

(also, this might also be part of the reason that increasing the chunk cache did not help last time we tried it)

issue_title: Incorrect indentation leads to lists wrapped in blockquote
issue_body:
These lists should not be indented. Because of the leading whitespace Sphinx wraps them in a blockquote.

http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_shapes.html (also choose better `figsize` to minimize whitespace)
http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_rank_mean.html
http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_radon_transform.html
http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_hog.html

For reference, this is how a list should be rendered sans blockquote. It is the only correctly rendered list in our example gallery.
http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/auto_examples/plot_medial_transform.html

I've selected for refactoring 11 lines of code which are duplicated in 1 file(s) ([1](https://github.com/PolyJIT/benchbuild/blob/52ec6f984169c158e9517fd6179ad4b6ded27eaf/benchbuild/experiments/pj_sequence.py#L334-L349), [2](https://github.com/PolyJIT/benchbuild/blob/52ec6f984169c158e9517fd6179ad4b6ded27eaf/benchbuild/experiments/pj_sequence.py#L585-L600)).  Addressing this will make our codebase more maintainable and improve [Better Code Hub](https://bettercodehub.com)'s **Write Code Once** guideline rating! 👍 

Here's the gist of this guideline:
- **Definition** 📖 
Do not copy code.
- **Why**❓ 
When code is copied, bugs need to be fixed in multiple places. This is both inefficient and a source of regression bugs.
- **How** 🔧 
Avoid duplication by never copy/pasting blocks of code and reduce duplication by extracting shared code, either to a new unit or introduce a superclass if the language permits.

You can find more info about this guideline in [Building Maintainable Software](http://shop.oreilly.com/product/0636920049159.do). 📖 
 
---- 
ℹ️ To know how many _other_ refactoring candidates need addressing to get a guideline compliant, select some by clicking on the 🔲  next to them. The risk profile below the candidates signals (✅) when it's enough! 🏁 


----
Good luck and happy coding! :shipit: :sparkles: :100:

issue_title: Conda environment using default packages instead of throwing an error and other unexpected behaviour
issue_body:
Hi all, I've been using conda environment for a while, but the recent version seems to have caused some new changes that can cause confusion. I've illustrated this below with an example.

Let's say I create an environment with Python2 called testing-env

```
> conda create -n testing-env python=2
Fetching package metadata .......
Solving package specifications: ..........

Package plan for installation in environment /Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/testing-env:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    openssl:    1.0.2h-2
    pip:        8.1.2-py27_0
    python:     2.7.12-1
    readline:   6.2-2
    setuptools: 27.2.0-py27_0
    sqlite:     3.13.0-0
    tk:         8.5.18-0
    wheel:      0.29.0-py27_0
    zlib:       1.2.8-3

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

Linking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|#############################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################################| 100%
#
# To activate this environment, use:
# > source activate testing-env
#
# To deactivate this environment, use:
# > source deactivate testing-env
#
```

I can activate this environment by the following.

```
> source activate testing-env
```

The python executable exists in the environment. This is good and expected.

```
(testing-env) > which python
/Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/testing-env/bin/python
```

But when I run the following command, I get the global jupyter executable. This is not expected. 
 
```
(testing-env) > which jupyter
/Users/$USER/anaconda/bin/jupyter
```

My first explanation of this was that jupyter was somehow preloaded with anaconda. But the same thing happens with nosetests and py.test!

```
(testing-env) > which nosetests
/Users/$USER/anaconda/bin/nosetests

(testing-env) > which py.test
/Users/$USER/anaconda/bin/py.test
```

It also finds packages I've installed like the one below!


```
(testing-env) > which fono
/Users/$USER/anaconda/bin/fono
```

Inside a new environment, I can now run jupyter notebook, import fono and everything would work completely fine and me as a user would be completely oblivious. 

Let's say I want to use a different version of jupyter, I can install jupyter inside the environment. 


```

(testing-env) > conda install jupyter -y
...

```

But on installing the environment, jupyter is still linked to the global environment.


```
(testing-env) > which jupyter
/Users/$USER/anaconda/bin/jupyter
```

If I deactivate and activate the environment, I now can access my installation within the environment.


```

(testing-env) > source deactivate

> source activate testing-env

(testing-env) > which jupyter
/Users/$USER/anaconda/envs/testing-env/bin/jupyter

```

This is planned behaviour for conda env? I find the first effect completely unintuitive. I propose the raising an `ImportError` if a user is in an environment.

It would be really convenient to be able to generate a `meta.yml`  w/ `conda skeleton` from:
- a GitHub URL
- a pypi package w/ a "Homepage URL" that matches `github.com` and/or `bitbucket.org/`

Use case: https://github.com/audreyr/cookiecutter/issues/232

```
conda skeleton -t python https://github.com/audreyr/cookiecutter/
```

Where `-t python` would hint that there should be a setup.py found in the root directory.

Sorry if I am not reporting this in the right place.

pyyaml is required for conda-env, as per discussion on gitter.

issue_title: Signatures are not secure or safe
issue_body:
Taking a look at the signature verification in conda, I see several issues which result in the signature system being fairly broken as is.

* Currently it's using 1024 bit RSA keys (according to [teoliphant](https://twitter.com/teoliphant/status/614173906358943744)). This is not a safe size for RSA keys, there is some indication that some nation state adversaries can already brute force a private key from a public key, and attacks only get better and faster. This should be at least 2048 if not 4096.
* It's using "Textbook" or "Raw" RSA. This is really bad and more or less breaks the security of the crypto system. The exact math behind it is opaque to me, but you can see it being discussed [here](http://rdist.root.org/2009/10/06/why-rsa-encryption-padding-is-critical/), [here](https://crypto.stackexchange.com/questions/20085/which-attacks-are-possible-against-raw-textbook-rsa), and many more papers and presentations from cryptographers about the topic if you google for it.

My suggestion would be to deprecate this feature immediately and try to attract a cryptographer or a security engineer to help design the secure system. In particular the folks behind [TUF](http://theupdateframework.com/) have been extremely helpful and will likely be the solution used by pip/PyPI. At the very least you should just use gpg or NaCl for signatures if you don't want to or can't find someone willing to help.

issue_title: Docs out of date for recommended install version.
issue_body:
The recommended install version (according to the README) is >=2.0.dev, however the docs have not been updated to reflect some breaking changes. Specifically http://docs.graphene-python.org/projects/django/en/latest/tutorial-plain/ still uses:
```python
def resolve_category(self, args, context, info):
```
This should be changed to 
```python
    def resolve_category(self, info, **args):
```
I would be glad to put a pull request in for this issue.

In GraphiQL it is all so easy:

```
query PendingUsers($id: Int!) {
  pending: pendingUsers(id: $id) {
    email
  }
}
```

query variables:
```
{"id": "6"}
```

How to get the same result in Python, using schema.execute()?


```python
query = '''
  query PendingUsers($id: Int!) {
    pending: pendingUsers(id: $id) {
      email
    }
  }
'''

schema.execute(query)
# => Variable "$id" of required type "Int!" was not provided.

schema.execute(query, {"id": 6}) 
# => Variable "$id" of required type "Int!" was not provided.

schema.execute(query, {"id": "6"})
# => Variable "$id" of required type "Int!" was not provided.

schema.execute(query, {"$id": "6"})
# => Variable "$id" of required type "Int!" was not provided.

schema.execute(query, {"$id": 6})
# => Variable "$id" of required type "Int!" was not provided.

schema.execute(query, id=6)
# TypeError: graphql() got an unexpected keyword argument 'id'
```

pro:

- less cluttered scripts

con:
- madseq loses its independence (currently its just a small python script that can be used without any compilation or setup)

issue_title: Default timeout should be None and block indefinitely
issue_body:
Defaulting the [fido.fetch(...) timeout](https://github.com/Yelp/fido/blob/master/fido/fido.py#L143) value to 1 second goes againt the [principle of least surpise](http://c2.com/cgi/wiki?PrincipleOfLeastSurprise).

The default should be `None` and the behavior should block indefinitely.

Others libs that follow this pattern for default timeouts:
- [Requests](https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/blob/587eb193ddbc2dfc10c2bdbf1718cd2267a0e77f/requests/sessions.py#L393)
- [concurrent.futures](https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.Future.result)
- [python socket stdlib](https://docs.python.org/2/library/socket.html#socket.getdefaulttimeout)

This breaks backwards compatibility in behavior so this should be a major version bump with the appropriate documentation.

issue_title: missing properties are not set to default when unmarshalled
session.login() returns True but subsequent calls fail if the server's session cookie name is not 'RETS-Session-ID'
issue_body:
It would be nice if login() threw an error in this case. 

I am working against a RETS server that does not use the standard session cookie name 'RETS-Session-ID', so while the login is "successful", the `session_id` is never set, so subsequent calls to other endpoints fail because the RETS-UA-Authorization header is incorrect due to the session_id not being included in the hash.

Following up with a PR to allow nonstandard cookie names, and punting on the error throwing from login().
Is there any example?

```

TypeError: 'instancemethod' object is not iterable
```

and this:

```
.../plone/app/contenttypes/migration/browser.py", line 402, in updateWidgets
    if not len(widget.items):
TypeError: object of type 'instancemethod' has no len()
```

This is caused by dd40f6f513b08854c64e8d59cf6440082f4b11d9.

Before this change, the `select` browser view was the only one with `items` as method.
After this change, the `orderedselect` browser view is the only one with `items` as property.

It would be nicer if all were the same, but it's not clear which one it should be...
Can we compromise and update the changed radio and checkbox browser view to use their new methods but add an `@property`?

Hi, being a beginner in using this client, I'd like more working examples as they are of invaluable value. Unfortunately this is not the case at the moment because various issues:

 * code seems broken/obsolete and definitely not tested (for example `spec = client.V1PodSpec()` in examples/notebooks/create_configmap.ipynb raise a `ValueError`)
 * despite [notebooks/README.md](https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/blob/369366a813964e3d72e91d402c5f471275e7a3e5/examples/notebooks/README.md), not every playbook can be run inside a pod because the config loading machinery being used (`load_kube` vs `load_cluster`)
 * not every examples/ script is available as a python notebook so it's not straightforward to run it under a jupyter pod (which is imho a great idea)

This in my opinion steeps up learning curve or at least does not help new users (as I also am) as it could.

What I'm proposing is:

1. rewrite/renew examples as a jupyter notebook, exploiting this technology in order to ease code development/maintenance
1. make scripts configuration automatically aware of the environment so we can run examples inside or outside a pod. It is a common pattern to ship docs/examples facilities just for the sake of simplicity:
    ```python
    # examples/load_config.py

    from kubernetes import config

    # Silly example! It could be better testing for env vars or files
    # and then working/failing accordingly
    try:
        config.load_incluster_config()
    except:
        config.load_kube_config()
    ```
1. automatically generate plain python example scripts with `jupyter nbconvert` so we dev on notebooks but we don't give up on distributing plain scripts
1. put everything under CI using `jupyter nbconvert --execute`

What do you think? I saw good efforts from other users too (ping @djkonro, @tomplus), could we maybe coordinate on this one? 


Thanks in advance, regards!

issue_title: Initial samples repeated when launching training
issue_body:
The training process has a set of queues that are filled by a set of threads that read the .wav data from disk and convert it to the required format. This is orchestrated by the `ModelFeeder`, and the threads start filling the queues after the session is created at line [1526](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1526):

```python
...
        def after_create_session(self, session, coord):
            log_debug('Starting queue runners...')
            model_feeder.start_queue_threads(session, coord)
            log_debug('Queue runners started.')
...
```

Each thread gets the index of the training example to process from the coordinator (`COORD`) by calling [next_index](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/util/feeding.py#L144),
 which is initialized to [get_next_index](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1332) when the `DataSet` are [created](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1468):


```python
...
    # Reading training set
    train_set = DataSet(FLAGS.train_files.split(','),
                        FLAGS.train_batch_size,
                        limit=FLAGS.limit_train,
                        next_index=lambda i: COORD.get_next_index('train'))
...
```

`get_next_index` references a set of `TrainingCoordinator` [variables](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1132):

```
...
class TrainingCoordinator(object):
...
    def _reset_counters(self):
        self._index_train = 0
        self._index_dev = 0
        self._index_test = 0

    def _init(self):
        self._epochs_running = []
        self._epochs_done = []
        self._reset_counters()
        self._dev_losses = []
...
```

The TensorFlow session is started, which causes the threads to start filling the queues due to the hook. After a period of time, the `TrainingCoordinator` is [started](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1588) which resets the index variables [to 0](https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech/blob/master/DeepSpeech.py#L1588):

```python
...
        with tf.train.MonitoredTrainingSession(...) as session:
...
                    COORD.start_coordination(model_feeder, step)
...
```

```python
...
class TrainingCoordinator(object):
...

...
            self._init()
...
```

The queues, however, are not flushed and still contain any values fetched by the threads in the that gap of time between thread starting and variable reset. These examples will therefore be used twice during the epoch and a number of training examples will also _not_ be fetched at the end of the epoch.

batch_seq_len = tf.Print(batch_seq_len, [batch_seq_len], message='D Batch Sequence Length: ')

issue_title: Prevent the deletion of stacks that receive actual traffic
issue_body:
The command `senza delete` by default prevents the deletion of stacks that *potentially* receive traffic. The check is based on DNS weights and does not check if the stack *actually* receives traffic. We repeatedly face the situation that stakeholders calling us use HTTP clients with long running connections and thus remain calling the old stack even though the DNS weight is set to `0` for a long time.

The actual traffic a.k.a request-count metric can be retrieved as CloudWatch metric for the stack's load-balancer. This solution is therefore limited to stacks having a load-balancer.

I added an endpoint to receive the request-count to lizzy [here](https://github.com/zalando-stups/lizzy/pull/223), but it was argued there that the functionality should be integrated into senza.

Before I start doing that, I want to align here and make sure we agree on a solution.

I start with the proposal to change the current default logic of `senza delete` by adding the requirement of the ELB's request-count being 0 for the last 5min. As before, this logic can be overridden by using `--force`. If the stack doesn't have a load-balancer the check doesn't apply.

Another solution could be to add a flag like `--ensure-no-requests` such that the check is only performed if the flag is present.

I would also like to get some feedback what you think on the time-interval to be checked. I think 5min is a responsable default. Having that configurable by the user might clutter the API.

What do you think?

issue_title: Why Can't There Be One .NET framework that's xplatform with all the bells.
issue_body:
Please don't take this wrong and I'm not trying to start a flame war but, It puzzles me why Microsoft has chosen to have multiple frameworks  other than the fact that the full framework is years in the making and obviously still in heavy use. But, Why in the world would you not have a roadmap to make a better framework that can eventually replace both the full windows only versions and mono. 

Clearly cross platform is the BOMB and There are plenty of API's that can be wrapped to provide Native UI and OS libraries that can be accessed in both Mac, Linux and mobile devices in addition to what works on windows. Just look at AvaloniaUI, ETO.Forms and Xamarin.Xwt and Xamarin.Forms all prove that a consistent method can provide desktop application and mobile capability to .NET that will be consistent looking native interfaces across major OS's including mobile devices. 

Why not capitalize on this concept port System.Xaml and System.Drawing and once and for all provide a cross platform solution that allows developers to get things done. Why can't we write apps on any OS that can work anywhere including IOT and mobile devices with one unified framework. 

Also it IMHO is a crime that Microsoft has not embraced the entire .NET ecosystem which would in my opinion provide them with a clear winner for example, there have been many of languages ported or created to run on the platform, Boo, Java, Python, Ruby, PHP, Cobra, Cobol, Typescript, Javascript and several others. It baffles me why Microsoft does not officially support these efforts so that .Net can attract more usage.

I suspect many developers of PHP or Java or Ruby would love to be able to maintain their skill set using the dotnet core as it provides many benefits and allows for seamless integration not to mention it opens up a whole lot of mature code that has been written over the years to the dotnet ecosystem. It actually makes me upset to see addins and plugins for these languages fall unmaintained and become not usable in monodevelop, sharpdevelop and even VS has seen some come and go. And, not because people don't use them mainly because the developers running the IDE projects chose to stop maintaining them. 

It would be awesome to be able to have multiple language projects using one framework to maximize the best of the entire development ecosystem not just one companies vision of languages. It's easier to convert a developer to the CLR if their language is supported then to tell them to switch to my language.

Now that .Net is opensource it would be wonderful to see these efforts united to bring the framework inline with what it should be (IMHO) and see these languages being ported to Roslyn and brought inline with the current modern direction and standards of the .net core as a whole. It's totally possible and as example php is currently being ported to the Roslyn stack and dotnet core it's called peachpie and is the natural evolution of the Phalanger project. 

How come nobody tries to embrace a community instead of just their vision? Why can't we use the .NET Foundation as a way to bring a team together that consist of these projects spearheaded by a common goal for example we have the ASP.Net team, The Roslyn Team the .NET Core team how come we can't extend an offer and official support to the developers of relevant projects like Boo, PeachPie, IronPython, IronRuby and the many others including ETO.Forms and AvaloniaUI to become a unified foundation with the goal of making dotnet core a defacto standard with all the love bells and jingles of a xplat framework that supports not just C# and VB.Net but all the other languages that have already been proven to run on the CLR with full development capabilities including a UI kit, Web, API, Mobile and IOT out the box. It's a shame...  

I can totally see this:

DotNet Foundation
    Roslyn Team
    .NET Core team
    ASP.NET Team
    GUI Team
    OS Integration Team
    Language Teams
       C# Team
       VB Team
       Python Team
       Ruby Team
       Etc...

By bringing these isolated projects together and defining standards and goals officially supported.

What better way to try and set the standard then thru the foundation.

And please don't treat Linux as a second class citizen make it so we can create iOS and Android apps there to it has already been proven that mac apps can be developed in windows and debugged on a Mac using remote technology same for developing mono apps on windows debugging in a Linux VM why not use this knowledge to allow a unified experience across all platforms why should Linux developers be deprived of being able to use  Xamarin tools it makes no sense conquer the game don't tease it. 

I here the talk of windows forms does not make a good UI kit for xplat apps and that is true but it does make sense for developers using windows and should be in the stack for just that reason other UI kits can be abstracted for use on other systems like has been done already. If windows forms are deeply tied to the win32 api as many suggest, mono's windows form implementation can be picked up ported and extended to come inline with production standards and has already been partially ported to dotnet core as a test. 

Sure i wouldn't want to run a winform app on my linux or mac box but i could code and test their and know it would run on windows any production deployment on Linux for me would most certainly use the GTK bindings and on Mac well that's been covered to by Xamarin. Bottom-line for me as a dotnet lover is bring it quit playing. And please if Microsoft embraced the projects that can offer a clear path to help push the new efforts to it's maximum and provided official support you would gain the man power needed to make all this happen just by supporting and consuming the projects already in motion. 

Just my opinion, i mean am i the only person thinking that now that Microsoft is embracing opensource and supports the dotnet foundation and has purchased Xamarin that creating a vibrant community and bringing  some of these isolated project teams under one theme all pushing to make the new core a truly available option for all developers regardless to their language roots and making it so we only have one cross platform framework complete with UI support would be a game changer.

icontrol.exceptions.iControlUnexpectedHTTPError: 400 Unexpected Error: Bad Request for uri: https://10.190.3.58:443/mgmt/tm/ltm/nat/
Text: u'{"code":400,"message":"\\"disabled\\" may not be specified with \\"enabled\\"","errorStack":[]}'

After further inspection, it was found that the method https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/81e35af626579f96d13879a4f02506decb421aef/f5/bigip/tm/ltm/nat.py#L96 sees disabled=False and then sets enabled=True, and 11.5.4 does not allow this. The appropriate behavior is that the method should only keep whatever setting should be True. If disabled=False, enabled should be True and that should be submitted.

issue_title: Verification not working against WebCrypto keys
issue_body:
This might as well be some large fuckup of mine.

What I did:

1) used WebCrypto to generate a keypair.
```
const keyOptions = {name: "ECDSA", namedCurve: "P-256"}
const signOptions = {name: "ECDSA", hash: {name: "SHA-256"}}
```

2) exported the public key as JWK:
```
{"crv":"P-256","ext":true,"key_ops":["verify"],"kty":"EC","x":"kudpCZSQcYKDOVlYAxfVyZc6NhPFKB2I6-3-Q14leqE","y":"MTynqg8ZTf2eKZ-YKsgGFg35S88De5wz5Tol4YMujHc"}
```

3) took a trivial challenge, `"a"`, converted it to a `new Uint8Array([ 97 ])` and signed with the private key. Got the following signature:
```
tOdwela6LqWd6S3acKgLKAX6VXNb3NcDN1v6RR0/nGa1t/eF6OZTSU6puq2px9r815kA4XrW5oOZDYxNL+VdAQ==
```

4) loaded the exported public key in Python:
```
key = jwk.JWK(**decoded_public_key)
```

5) created a JWS token:
```
sig = jws.JWS()
```

6) tried to verify the signature:
```
>>> sig.deserialize('{"payload":"YQ==", "signature":"tOdwela6LqWd6S3acKgLKAX6VXNb3NcDN1v6RR0/nGa1t/eF6OZTSU6puq2px9r815kA4XrW5oOZDYxNL+VdAQ=="}', key, "ES256")
```

7) got the `InvalidSignature()` exception.

Is there something I am doing fundamentally incorrect?

issue_title: When creating a canonical list, can non-string values be combined in various ways?
issue_body:
Examples:
1. If a field is a list of strings (eg. tags), the canonical field value could be either a de-duped union of the lists, or the intersection.
2. Numeric values could produce the mean (or median).
3. Numeric values could produce a range (highest and lowest values seen)
4. Numeric _ranges_ (eg. 60-70) could produce a range where the high and low values are medians or means
5. Geo coordinates could produce the centroid.

Etc.

For my use case I am most interested in the first four.

issue_title: Proposal: auto-commit bot for allocating operator version numbers
issue_body:
At the moment, operator set versions are a global monotonically increasing number which otherwise has no structure. The problem I have noticed is that people keep forgetting to update the version number when they make changes.

I propose that we create a commit bot to automatically allocate operator numbers for users. Here is how it will work:

1. Instead of setting `SinceVersion(3)` with a specific number, users will instead commit and push `SinceVersion(UNALLOCATED_VERSION)`. This sigil will be some variable whose value tells ONNX that the version number is unallocated
2. A bot monitors the repo for commits which reference `UNALLOCATED_VERSION` in the diff. When it detects such a commit, it generates and pushes a commit replacing this placeholder with a real version (also regenerating the docs)

What do people think?

CC @donbox

issue_title: Time range Query created with Datacube is incorrect
issue_body:
When creating a query using the datacube query API, requesting 2 element time range from `a` to `b` is resulting in a query with a time range from `a` to `a`
See: https://github.com/opendatacube/datacube-core/blob/ef03ad272ce247d71a439cdc58934be6cca82e8b/datacube/api/query.py#L277

### Expected behaviour
```
In [19]: dt0 = datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 1)

In [20]: dt1 = datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 10)

In [21]: datacube.api.query.Query(time=(dt0, dt1))
Out[21]: 
Datacube Query:
        type = None
        search = {'time': Range(begin=datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>), end=datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 10, 0, 0, 0, 999999, tzinfo=tzutc()))}
        geopolygon = None
```

### Actual behaviour

```
In [5]: dt0 = datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 1)

In [6]: dt1 = datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 10)

In [7]: datacube.api.query.Query(time=(dt0, dt1))
Out[7]: 
Datacube Query:
        type = None
        search = {'time': Range(begin=datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 1, 0, 0, tzinfo=<UTC>), end=datetime.datetime(2018, 6, 1, 0, 0, 0, 999999, tzinfo=tzutc()))}
        geopolygon = None
```

issue_title: 通过openid获取用户昵称返回的编码感觉有错

issue_body: scripts like `ppserver.py` should not use the `.py` extension, however this may require some special handling on windows.

Launch in a shell the following command :
```
$ ppserver.py -a
```
Now try to launch sum_primes.py example with '127.0.0.1' in ppservers tuple.
```
$ python sum_primes.py 0
```
It just freeze while with the original pp version, it works.
In order to get ppft to work with sum_primes.py example you have to specify which port to use and listen on.
```
$ ppserver.py -a -p60000
```
and '127.0.0.1:60000' in ppservers tuple.

> 2. Go to manage tab
> 3. Try to find the "channel" icon and notice that you can't see it in portrait"

We may want to investigate a strategy for making sub-nav items more discoverable.

----

One option might be to include sub-sections for the currently top-level section in the side nav, e.g.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/28536095-714b4b70-705b-11e7-8833-990cc2da7d36.png)

issue_title: poor user-facing handling of incomplete channel imports
issue_body:
For typical scenarios, a user sees the following chain of events on channel import:

* Manage
  * initiate channel import
  * see progress indicator
  * channel appears in channel list
* Learn
  * channel appears in switcher
  * content is available to be browsed

However, it is possible to deviate from this flow in a couple ways:

 * User refreshes or opens Manage in a new tab. They will see the new channel in the list, with no indication that it's only partially imported.
 * Another user browses Learn while import is in progress. They will see the new channel, but fail to load content with 404s

We need to better handle "partially imported" channels for both learners and admins.

issue_title: Open Source math homework project interested in collaborating
issue_body:
Hello Kolibri Team!

I am the developer of Free Math, an offline web application for completing step-by-step math homework digitally, and a grading interface for quickly processing an entire classes work to find where students are misunderstanding material.

The app is deployed here: http://freemathapp.org/

While the system doesn't provide instant feedback, it does offer an opportunity for learners to convey their current understanding in a format easily sharable with their teachers. This enables teachers and coaches the unique opportunity to see what specific mistakes are made on each assignment by different groups of students on each question.

Currently the application is designed to be LMS agnostic by working with files, but it looks like Kolibri is structured around individual exercises that are linked together in the web app, and may not have a facility for collecting files right now.

I am open to changing the structure of Free Math to accommodate integrating it with Kolibri. An initial integration could simply be an optional workspace for students to use when completing their math work. This work generated by students could be saved, and incorrect work could be shown to coaches when an incorrect answer is submitted.

I do like the opportunity for teachers to provide partial credit based on the work as a benefit of Free Math, but this currently requires making feedback asynchronous, which is a drawback compared to instant fully-automated grading.

Question on parameter ranges that I know you have hinted at in other issues, but as far as I can tell haven't directly addressed. I am also referencing the following link: 

https://github.com/nmslib/nmslib/blob/master/python_bindings/parameters.md

Elsewhere you mentioned it is possible to go above the values listed at the link for m,efc,efs. Is there a limit to how far above these values you can go? Is this dictated by your data set size? I am working on a 500k x 20k sparse matrix and would like to improve my results in terms of getting closer to the true nearest neighbors. I had previously run the query with the following parameters:
m=100
efc=2000
efs=2000

I also recently read in the documentation about the post=2 parameter, which I will add on my next run. I'm just trying to make sure I understand all my options for improvement.

Hi,
    I'm using the `bucket_module`, and in `mod.fit()` when I first `bind`(using default_bucket_key) it works well, but when the `bucket_key` changes, in `switch bucket`, some error happens. I debug it and find in `symbol.simple_bind`, the `_LIB.MXExecutorSimpleBind`(Line 1453) throws error:'std:out of range,what():  _Map_base::at', I list the parameters of function `simple_bind` as follow:
```

shared_data_arrays:{'data':mx.nd.zeros((2,3,640,640)),'expression':mx.nd.zeros((2,50),'label':mx.nd.zeros((2,1,5))}
```
I'm confused and struggle for this question for some days, any help will be appreciated.
**Thanks**

issue_title: Paradox VRAM demand as a function of batch size: Low batch size, high VRAM demand
issue_body:
Dear community,

I'm running mxnet on the environment as mentioned below with the following hardware:
- 32 GB RAM
- 8 x NVIDIA 1080 Ti, 8 GB VRAM
- 12 CPUs

MXnet is utlized to train a stacked denoising autoencoder reconstruction of an array with dimension about 62.000 x 100.

I was looking for a batch size with minimum VRAM demand. Therefore I measured the VRAM demand per single Card for a given combination of "number of GPUs" and "array.batch.size".

The results are shown in the attached table:
Columns indicate array.batch.size and rows indicate number of GPUs.
Each entry indicates the GPU memory usage per Card.

![paradox_vram_batchsize](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/29228225/29312390-5b2b6c60-81b5-11e7-9258-f877551427e1.jpg)

As one can see if array.batch.size is below or above a particular value, VRAM demand rises.
Given an array.batch.size of 12, computation crashes due to "cuda memory allocation error" (red style).
Given a array.batch.size of 128 (2 and 4 GPUS) or 256 (6) results in a minimum VRAM demand of about 3GB per card (green style).

My question is:
**Why does VRAM hunger rise below a particular array.batch.size value?**

Class A with K instances
ClassB with K/2 instances

issue_title: How can I apply scalar multiplication on a specific dimension of a symbol?
issue_body:
How can I apply scalar multiplication on a specific dimension of a symbol, but without slice the tensor?

For example, I got the symbol **Conv1** with size `[128, 64, 32, 32]` and variable **c** with size `[64]`

How can I apply **c[i]** scalar multiplication with **Conv1** `[128, i, 32, 32]`?
That is, apply the variable **c** scalar multiplication on `axis=1` with **Conv1**.

I know 'full group' 1x1 group convolution is result equivalent, however it is too slow.
Thanks for reading this.
